Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

IMPLICIT BIAS OF PROJECTED SUBGRADIENT METHOD GIVES PROVABLE ROBUST RECOVERY OF SUBSPACES OF UNKNOWN CODIMENSION
**Paris Giampouras, Benjamin D. Haeffele and René Vidal** Mathematical Institute for Data Science Johns Hopkins University Baltimore, MD, USA {parisg,bhaeffele,rvidal}@jhu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Robust subspace recovery (RSR) is the problem of learning a subspace from sample data points corrupted by outliers. Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) is a robust subspace recovery method that aims to find a basis for the orthogonal complement of the subspace by minimizing the sum of the distances of the points to the subspaces subject to orthogonality constraints on the basis. Prior work has shown that DPCP can provably recover the correct subspace in the presence of outliers as long as the true dimension of the subspace is known. In this paper, we show that if the orthogonality constraints –adopted in previous DPCP formulations– are relaxed and random initialization is used instead of spectral one, DPCP can provably recover a subspace of _unknown dimension_ . Specifically, we propose a very simple algorithm based on running multiple instances of a projected sub-gradient descent method (PSGM), with each problem instance seeking to find one vector in the null space of the subspace. We theoretically prove that under mild conditions this approach succeeds with high probability. In particular, we show that 1) all of the problem instances will converge to a vector in the nullspace of the subspace and 2) the ensemble of problem instance solutions will be sufficiently diverse to fully span the nullspace of the subspace thus also revealing its true unknown codimension. We provide empirical results that corroborate our theoretical results and showcase the remarkable implicit rank regularization behavior of the PSGM algorithm that allows us to perform RSR without knowing the subspace dimension.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Robust subspace recovery (RSR) refers to the problem of identifying an underlying linear subspace (with dimension less than the ambient data dimension) from sample data points that are potentially corrupted with outliers (i.e., points that do not lie in the linear subspace). Many methods for RSR have been proposed in the literature over the past several years (Xu et al., 2012; You et al., 2017a; Lerman & Maunu, 2018). Formulations based on convex relaxations and decompositions of the data matrix into a low-rank matrix plus a matrix of sparse corruptions – either entrywise-sparse corruptions as in Candès et al. (2011) or columnwise-sparse corruptions as in Xu et al. (2012); McCoy & Tropp (2011) – can, in certain situations, be shown to provably recover the true subspace when the dimension is unknown. However, these theoretical guarantees often require the dimension of the subspace, _d_ , to be significantly less than the ambient dimension of the data, _D_ , and these methods are not suitable for the more challenging regime of subspaces of high relative dimension (i.e., when _D[d][≈]_[1][).]

**Dual Principal Component Pursuit.** Recently, progress has been made towards solving the RSR problem in the high relative dimension regime by a formulation termed Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP). As implied by its name, DPCP follows a _dual_ perspective of RSR by aiming to recover a basis for the orthogonal complement of the inliers’ subspace. As shown in (Tsakiris & Vidal, 2018), DPCP is provably robust in recovering subspaces of high relative dimension. However, a key limitation of DPCP is that it requires _a priori_ knowledge of the true subspace dimension.

DPCP was first formulated by Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) for handling subspaces of codimension _c_ = _D − d_ equal to 1 (i.e., the subspace is a hyperplane with dimension _d_ = _D −_ 1). In this case, DPCP is formulated as the optimization problem

which is nonconvex due to the spherical constraint imposed on the normal vector _**b** ∈_ S _[D][−]_[1] of the _D −_ 1 dimensional hyperplane. Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) showed that the global minimizer of (1) is a normal vector of the underlying true hyperplane when both inliers and outliers are well-distributed _or_ the ratio between the number of inliers and number of outliers is sufficiently small. Following a probabilistic point of view, (Zhu et al., 2018) presented an improved theoretical analysis of DPCP giving further insights on the remarkable robustness of DPCP in recovering the true underlying subspaces even in datasets heavily corrupted by outliers. Moreover, the authors introduced a projected subgradient method which converges to a normal vector of the true subspace at a linear rate.

**Recursive DPCP for known** _c >_ 1. Zhu et al. (2018) also proposed an extension to DPCP to subspaces with codimension _c >_ 1 via a projected subgradient algorithm that tries to learn _c_ normal vectors to the subspace in a recursive manner. Specifically, after convergence to a normal vector, the projected subgradient algorithm is initialized with a vector _orthogonal_ to the previously estimated normal vector. However, for that approach to be successful, knowledge of the true subspace codimension _c_ becomes critical. Specifically, if an underestimate of the true codimension _c_ is assumed the recovered basis for the null space, _**B**_[ˆ] , will fail to span the whole null space, _S⊥_ . On the other hand, an overestimate of _c_ will lead to columns of _**B**_[ˆ] corresponding to vectors that lie in _S_ .

**Orthogonal DPCP for known** _c_ . Zhu et al. (2019) proposed an alternative to (1) which attempts to solve for _c_ normal vectors to the subspace at once by minimizing the sum of the distances from the points to the subspace:

The authors also proposed an optimization algorithm based on the projected Riemannian subgradient method (RSGM), which builds on similar ideas as the projected subgradient method of Zhu et al. (2018) and enjoys a linear converge rate when the step size is selected based on a geometrically diminishing rule. Ding et al. (2021) provided a geometric analysis of (2) which shows the merits of DPCP in handling a) datasets highly contaminated by outliers (in the order of _M_ = _O_ ( _N_[2] )) and b) subspaces of high relative dimension. However, a key shortcoming of this approach is that, because all minimizers of (2) are orthogonal matrices, a prerequisite for recovering the correct orthogonal complement of the inliers subspace is the _a priori_ knowledge of the true codimension _c_ (see Fig. 1).

**Contributions.** In this work, we address this key limitation by proposing a framework that allows us to perform robust subspace recovery in the high relative subspace dimension regime **without** requiring _a priori_ knowledge of the true subspace dimension. In particular, our proposed approach is based on the simple idea of solving multiple, parallel instances of the DPCP formulation for subspaces of codimension one,

where _c[′]_ is assumed to be an upper bound of _c_ , i.e., _c[′] ≥ c_ . Contrary to (2), the objective function in (3) decouples over the columns _**b** i_ of matrix _**B**_ = [ _**b**_ 1 _**b**_ 2 _· · ·_ _**b** c′_ ] and thus can be solved in a parallel manner by independently applying a projected subgradient algorithm (referred to as PSGM) from _c[′]_ different _random initializations_ . Moreover, we observe that with random initialization we can get vectors sufficiently spread on the sphere that lead PSGM (initialized with those vectors) to return normal vectors of _S_ . These are all _linearly independent_ when _c[′] ≤ c_ and thus can span _S⊥_ when _c[′]_ = _c_ . If _c[′] > c_ then PSGM will return _c[′] − c_ redundant vectors that will still lie in _S⊥_ yet they will be linearly dependent (see Figure 1). That being said, we show that this simple strategy permits us to robustly recover the true subspace even without knowledge of the true codimension _c_ .

As is detailed in Sections 3 and 4, this remarkable behavior of PSGM originates from the _implicit bias_ that is induced in the optimization problem due to a) the relaxation of _orthogonality constraints_ in (3) and b) the _random initialization_ scheme that is adopted. Our specific contributions are as follows:

Second, we analyze the discrete version which is more challenging, yet of more practical interest, showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a _scaled_ and _perturbed_ version of the initial matrix _**B**_[0] . ## 2 RELATED WORK

**Subspace Recovery.** Learning underlying low-dimensional subspace representations of data has been a central topic of interest in machine learning research. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) has been the most celebrated method of this kind and is based on the minimization of the perpendicular distances of the data points from the estimates linear subspace, Jolliffe & Cadima (2016). Albeit, it is originally formulated as nonconvex optimization problem, PCA can be easily solved in closed form using a singular value decomposition (SVD) operation (see e.g. Vidal et al. (2016)). Despite its great success, PCA is prone to failure when handling datasets that contain _outliers_ i.e., data points whose deviation from the inliers’ subspace is “large” in the _ℓ_ 2 norm sense.

**Robust Subspace Recovery (RSR).** To remedy this weakness of PCA _robust subspace recovery (RSR)_ methods attempt to identify the outliers in the dataset an recover the true underlying lowdimensional subspace of the inliers Lerman & Maunu (2018); Maunu et al. (2019). A classical approach to this problem is RANSAC Fischler & Bolles (1981), which is given a time budget and randomly chooses per iteration _d_ points and then fits a _d_ -dimensional subspace to those points and checks how the proposed subspace fits the remaining data points. RANSAC then outputs the subspace that agrees with the largest number of points. However, RANSAC’s reliance on randomly sampling points to propose subspaces can be highly inefficient when the number of outliers is high (as well as the fact that RANSAC also needs knowledge of the true subspace dimension, _d_ ). The need to tackle inherent shortcomings of RANSAC pertaining to computational complexity issues inspired alternative convex formulations of RSR, Xu et al. (2012); You et al. (2017b); Rahmani & Atia (2017); Zhang & Lerman (2014). In Xu et al. (2012) the authors decompose the data matrix as a sum of a low-rank and a column-sparse component. However, theoretical guarantees obtained for convex

formulations only hold for subspaces of relatively low-dimensional subspaces i.e., for _d ≪ D_ where _d_ and _D_ denote the subspace and the ambient dimension, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, existing RSR algorithms rely heavily on one of two key assumptions. 1) The subspace is very low-dimensional relative to the ambient dimension ( _d ≪ D_ ) or 2) _The subspace dimension is a priori known_ . Undoubtedly, the second hypothesis is rather strong in real-world applications, and many applications also do not satisfy the first assumption. Moreover, heuristic strategies for selecting the dimension of the subspace are hard to be applied in the RSR setting since they incur computationally prohibitive procedures, Lerman & Maunu (2018).

**Relation to Orthogonal Dictionary Learning (ODL).** Note that objective functions in the form of (3) show up beyond RSR problems i.e., in orthogonal dictionary learning (ODL), sparse blind deconvolution, etc., Qu et al. (2020). Specifically, based on a similar formulation the authors in Bai et al. (2019) proved that _c[′]_ = _O_ ( _c_ log _c_ ) independent random initial vectors suffice in order to recover with high probability a dictionary of size _D × c_ with high accuracy. In this paper we aim to recover a basis of the orthogonal complement of a subspace of unknown dimension instead of accurately estimating a dictionary hence our goal differs from that in Bai et al. (2019).

**Implicit bias in Robust Recovery Problems.** The notions of implicit bias and implicit regularization have been used interchangeably in the nonconvex optimization literature for describing the tendency of optimization algorithms to converge to global minima of minimal complexity with favorable generalization properties in overparameterized models, Gunasekar et al. (2018). In the context of robust recovery, the authors in You et al. (2020) showed that Robust PCA can be suitably reparametrized in such a way to favor low-rank and sparse solutions without using any explicit regularization. In this work, we use the term implicit bias for describing the convergence of DPCPPSGM to low-rank solutions, which are not necessarily global minimizers, that span the orthogonal complement of the subspace when a) orthogonality constraints in DPCP formulation are relaxed b) DPCP is overparameterized i.e., _c[′] ≥ c_ and c) PSGM randomly initialized.

## 4 DUAL PRINCIPAL COMPONENT PURSUIT IN SUBSPACES OF UNKNOWN CODIMENSION

Current theoretical results provide guarantees for recovering the true inlier subspace, when the proposed algorithms know _a priori_ of the subspace codimension _c_ , which is a rather strong requirement and is far from being true in real word applications. Here we describe our proposed approach, which consists of removing the orthogonality constraint on _**B**_ , along with a theoretical analysis that gives guarantees of recovering the true underlying subspace even when the true codimension _c_ is unknown. First we analyze a continuous version of DPCP, which arises when the number of inliers and outliers are distributed according to continuous measures and their number tends to _∞_ . The continuous DPCP incurs an optimization problem with a benign landscape that allows us to better illustrate the favorable properties of DPCP-PSGM when it comes to the convergence of its iterates. Then we extend the results to the discrete case that deals with a finite number of inliers and outliers yielding a more challenging optimization landscape.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a theoretically grounded and practically motivated extension of Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) for robust subspace recovery. Its primary contribution—showing that relaxing orthogonality constraints and using random initialization allows the recovery of subspaces with unknown codimension—is innovative and addresses a significant limitation in existing RSR literature. The mathematical derivation connecting the continuous objective to the discrete PSGM iterates is elegant, demonstrating a clear 'implicit bias' toward low-rank solutions. While the methodology is sound, there are minor technical ambiguities regarding the adaptive step-size rule for multiple instances and the precise definition of the 'distance' metric used in the evaluation.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling contribution to robust subspace recovery by showing how relaxation of orthogonality constraints, coupled with random initialization and projected subgradient descent, enables recovery of subspaces of unknown codimension. The theoretical analysis is thorough, combining continuous and discrete settings with convergence guarantees grounded in the implicit bias of the optimization dynamics. The empirical results convincingly support the claims. However, there are several technical issues—particularly around the derivation and presentation of key equations—that raise concerns about correctness and reproducibility, especially in the discrete case analysis. Most critical is an apparent sign error in a subgradient update that propagates through later derivations. While the core idea is promising and well-motivated, these technical flaws, particularly in Section 4.2, reduce confidence in the theoretical guarantees as currently stated.

comments.title: Sign error in discrete subgradient update
comments.explanation: The subgradient update in Algorithm 1 and Equation (13) writes the update as subtracting the term µ[k] i (M o[k] bˆ i + N x[k] bˆ i). However, from the objective in (3), which is ∑_{j=1}^N |x_j^⊤ b_i| + ∑_{j=1}^M |o_j^⊤ b_i|, the subgradient with respect to b_i is ∑ sgn(x_j^⊤ b_i) x_j + ∑ sgn(o_j^⊤ b_i) o_j = N x_{b_i} + M o_{b_i}, as defined in the paragraph above (13). Since the algorithm minimizes this objective, the gradient descent step should be b_i ← b_i - µ (N x_{b_i} + M o_{b_i}). However, in both (3) and the surrounding discussion, this is written with a minus sign on the full gradient, but in the update, the signs of both terms are positive inside the parentheses, suggesting subtraction of a positive quantity. This would be correct only if the objective were being maximized. A careful reader might suspect a typo in the sign of the gradient term. However, in (13), the update is rewritten as b_i[k+1] = (1 - µ[k] i M cD) b_i[k] + ... which suggests a shrinking step toward the origin, which contradicts the expected behavior. Given that the continuous analysis assumes convergence to the correct subspace via scaling of projections, this sign error undermines the entire derivation in Section 4.2. The correct form should likely be subtracting a term involving the average inlier and outlier contributions, but as written, the direction is reversed. This error propagates into the projection analysis in (14) and the recursive expression (15), casting doubt on Lemma 6 and Theorem 7.

comments.title: Mismatch between objective and subgradient form
comments.explanation: The objective in (3) is stated as minimizing the sum of ℓ1 norms. The subgradient with respect to b_i is indeed the sum of the subgradients of |x_j^⊤ b_i| and |o_j^⊤ b_i|, which are sgn(x_j^⊤ b_i) x_j and sgn(o_j^⊤ b_i) o_j respectively. The definitions of x_{b_i} and o_{b_i} are correct. However, the update rule (13) subtracts the sum (M o_{b_i} + N x_{b_i}), which is consistent with minimizing a convex function. But a concern arises in Equation (14), where the projected update includes a term like (1 - µ M cD) PS⊥(b_i[k]), suggesting contraction. For this to make sense, cD must be positive, which it is (as average height of hemisphere), but the contraction coefficient being less than 1 requires the step size to be small. However, the expression in (13) includes both the inlier and outlier terms with the same sign in the subtraction, whereas intuitively, only outlier-related terms should push b_i toward S⊥. The inlier term x_{b_i} is meant to be eliminated via its projection, but as formulated, both terms contribute additively to the descent direction. This raises a conceptual issue: if x_{b_i} is aligned with S, and b_i is near S⊥, then x_{b_i} should be small, but the gradient still includes a component from inliers that might not vanish as rapidly. The mismatch is not in the formula per se but in whether the subgradient correctly separates roles. The derivation in (14) assumes a clean separation, but the update (13) does not reflect that. The sign error noted earlier exacerbates this confusion.

comments.title: Incorrect expression for limiting normal vectors in Lemma 4
comments.explanation: The expression in Lemma 4 for the limiting vector _**b**_* i is written as normalized PS⊥(PS⊥(_**b**_[0]_i)), which is just normalized PS⊥(_**b**_[0]_i), since PS⊥ is a projection. The double PS⊥ is a typo. But more problematically, the formula suggests that the limit is exactly the normalized projection of the initial vector, scaled by a norm. However, from the recurrence in (11), we have PS⊥(b_i[k+1]) = (1 - µ[k] i p cD) PS⊥(b_i[k]), so the projection shrinks geometrically. Thus, PS⊥(b_i[k]) → 0 unless µ[k] is summable and the product converges to a nonzero value. But in Zhu et al. (2018), the step size rule ensures convergence to a unit norm vector in S⊥. The correct behavior should involve the iterates aligning with S⊥ but renormalized at each step due to the spherical projection. The expression in (11) only tracks the projection, not the full vector. The correct conclusion should be that b_i[k] converges to PS⊥(_**b**_[0]_i) / ||PS⊥(_**b**_[0]_i)||, not as stated. The current form implies a norm that depends on the cumulative scaling, but the spherical projection resets the norm. This is a serious flaw in the derivation chain: the projection dynamics alone do not determine the limit because the radial component is renormalized. The authors likely meant to say that the direction aligns with PS⊥(_**b**_[0]_i), but the formal expression is incorrect.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of average inliers and outliers
comments.explanation: The definitions of _**x_b_i** and _**o_b_i** are central to the discrete analysis. The use of Sgn with capital 'S' suggests the subgradient of the absolute value, which is standard. However, the symbol is rendered inconsistently—sometimes 'Sgn', sometimes 'sgn' elsewhere—as a result of OCR, but assuming 'Sgn' is meant to be the sign function (with values -1, 0, +1). The key issue is that when b_i is orthogonal to an inlier x_j, the sign is undefined (0), and its contribution is zero. This is correct. But the average is taken only over the current batch, and in Algorithm 1, these terms are recomputed at each iteration based on the current b_i[k]. The notation _**x**^[k]_bˆ_i and _**o**^[k]_bˆ_i is used later, but the initial definition lacks iteration dependence. This omission makes the update rule ambiguous: is the average computed only once or at each step? The algorithm pseudocode suggests it is computed per iterate, but the text does not emphasize this. Given that the subgradient depends on the current b_i, this is essential. While this is likely clear to experts, the lack of explicit time-indexing in the definition could mislead a careful reader into thinking these are fixed quantities, which would invalidate the algorithm. The issue is resolved by the algorithm description, but the standalone definition is underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistency in convergence claim vs. actual output
comments.quote: The PSGM iterates ... will almost surely asymptotically converge to a matrix _**B**_[ˆ] _[∗] ∈_ R _[D][×][c][′] whose columns _**b**_[ˆ] _[∗] i will be normal vectors of the inliers’ subspace
comments.explanation: Lemma 3 claims that all columns of _**B*** converge to normal vectors in S⊥. However, if c' > c, then the space S⊥ has dimension c < c', so it is impossible for c' vectors in S⊥ to be linearly independent. The convergence of each *individual* column to S⊥ is still possible, but the lemma makes no mention of linear dependence or rank collapse. In fact, the next paragraph acknowledges this by stating that if c' > c, redundant (dependent) vectors are returned. However, Lemma 3 as stated does not qualify this: it says all vectors converge to normal vectors (true), but does not say that they span S⊥ or are linearly independent. The issue is that 'normal vectors of the subspace' is ambiguous—it could mean any vector in S⊥, not necessarily basis elements. But the subsequent claim about span requires linear independence. The lemma is technically correct as stated, but the lack of qualification misleads the reader into thinking full recovery is immediate. A better statement would be that each column converges to some vector in S⊥, and with probability one, the ensemble spans S⊥ when c' ≥ c. The current wording overstates the lemma's strength.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption on limit existence in discrete case
comments.quote: where _**δ**_[ˆ] _i_ = lim _K→∞_ _**δ** i[K]_ 2. Following the same steps
comments.explanation: The derivation assumes the existence of the limit lim_{K→∞} δ_i[K] without justification. While Theorem 1 guarantees that b_i[K] converges to some b_* ∈ S⊥ ∩ S^{D-1}, the expression for δ_i[K] is defined recursively in (15) as a sum involving all previous steps. The existence of this limit depends on the convergence of the perturbation series, which in turn relies on the step size rule and the behavior of noise terms e_X and e_O. The paper states in a footnote that existence is assumed for brevity and refers to Lemma 8 in the appendix, which is not provided. Without access to the appendix, this step appears unjustified. For a major claim like Theorem 7, the convergence of the perturbation term should be established or at least discussed. This is a non-trivial step, especially because the errors e_X and e_O depend on the iterates and may not be summable. The assumption is plausible under the problem geometry and diminishing step size, but as presented, it's a significant gap.

comments.explanation: I initially thought this might be a simple indexing slip, but the contradiction is systematic. The main text explicitly assigns the unknown-codimension (proposed method) result to the middle panel and the known-codimension (baseline) result to the left panel. The figure caption swaps these, stating: 'DPCP-PSGM algorithm provided an overestimated of the true c i.e., c' = 10 (left), RSGM provided the true c (middle)'. Since the left panel visually demonstrates the core contribution (robustness without knowing c), misassigning it to a baseline method in the caption misleads the reader about which experiment validates the proposed framework. This should be corrected to match the text.

comments.title: Inconsistent geometric constant: c_{O,max} converges to c_D vs c_d
comments.quote: condition (18) is trivially satisfied (RHS of (18) tends to 0 since \eta_O \to 0 and c_{O,max} \to c_d )
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the authors correctly define $c_{O,\max}$ and $c_{O,\min}$ for outliers distributed in the ambient space $\mathbb{R}^D$, stating they converge to $c_D$ (the average height of a unit hemisphere in $\mathbb{R}^D$). However, in the discussion of Theorem 7, the text claims convergence to $c_d$ (the constant for the $d$-dimensional inlier subspace). Since the error term $\eta_O$ and the outlier geometry depend on the ambient distribution, the limit should consistently be $c_D$. This typo may cause confusion about whether the probabilistic bounds scale with the ambient dimension $D$ or the subspace dimension $d$.

comments.title: Incorrect normalization factor in definition of \eta_X
comments.quote: \eta_X = \frac{1}{M} \max_{\mathbf{b} \in S^{D-1}} \| (\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp} - \mathbf{b}\mathbf{b}^\top) \mathbf{X}_{\text{Sgn}(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{b})} \|_2.
comments.explanation: The parameter $\eta_X$ measures the discrepancy of the inlier term and is defined with a $1/M$ scaling factor. However, $\mathbf{X}$ contains $N$ inlier columns, and the subsequent claim states '$\eta_X \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$'. For the probabilistic scaling to be consistent with the number of inliers, the factor should be $1/N$. Using $1/M$ incorrectly ties the inlier statistics to the number of outliers and breaks the parallel structure with the $1/M$ scaling used for $\eta_O$. This is likely a typographical error in the manuscript.

comments.title: Non-standard singular value notation in Lemma 6
comments.quote: If \sigma_{c'}( \mathbf{B}_0 ) > \|\hat{\mathbf{\Delta}}\|_2 then matrix \mathbf{A} = \mathbf{B}_0 - \hat{\mathbf{\Delta}} is full-rank.
comments.explanation: The notation $\sigma_{c'}(\mathbf{B}_0)$ is ambiguous and non-standard. Singular values are conventionally indexed in descending order ($\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_{\min}$), so $\sigma_{c'}$ would denote the $c'$-th singular value rather than the smallest one. The following sentence clarifies that a 'lower-bound on the smallest singular is satisfied', indicating the authors intend $\sigma_{\min}(\mathbf{B}_0)$. Using standard notation would prevent confusion, especially since $c'$ is a variable overparameterization parameter that changes across experiments.

comments.title: Scaling factor discrepancy in discrete projection recursion (Eq. 15)
comments.quote: ( 1 - \mu^j_i M c_D ) where for j > K - 1 we set...
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1, the continuous analysis shows that the $S^\perp$ component of the iterates is scaled by a factor of $(1 - \mu c_D)$ per step. Equation (15) for the discrete version introduces a factor of $M c_D$. Unless the step size $\mu^k_i$ is implicitly defined to include a $1/M$ scaling (which is not stated), this factor is dimensionally inconsistent with the continuous limit and the standard DPCP subgradient (which contains a $1/M$ for outliers). It is highly likely that a $1/M$ was accidentally omitted or moved, and adding it would align the discrete recursion with the continuous derivation.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper makes a valuable contribution by demonstrating that the implicit bias of the projected subgradient method (PSGM) allows for robust subspace recovery without knowing the subspace codimension, relaxing a major limitation of previous DPCP methods. The theoretical approach is conceptually sound, leveraging the statistical geometry of the sphere and standard results on permeance statistics. However, the review identifies several significant issues: a critical error in Lemma 4 where a vector is equated to a scalar; a derivation in Section 4.2 (Equation 11) that relies on an unstated alignment property of the gradient terms to maintain a simple scaling relationship; and a persistent notation inconsistency in the definition of permeance statistics (using $d-1$ instead of $D-1$). Additionally, an assumption statement is mathematically incomplete due to a missing denominator. While these errors are specific and localized, they should be corrected to ensure the rigor of the proofs.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel DPCP-based robust subspace recovery method that relaxes orthogonality constraints and uses random initialization to handle unknown subspace codimension. The continuous analysis is clean and provides valuable intuition about implicit bias. However, the discrete analysis, which is the practically relevant part, contains significant gaps. The recursive expression (16) for the projected iterates appears unjustified and likely incorrect due to improper handling of normalization and additive perturbations. Theorem 7, which depends on this recursion, consequently presents an incomplete and potentially erroneous sufficient condition, undermining the paper's main theoretical contribution. Additionally, several ambiguities in notation (e.g., unused φ_i, undefined M,N in the continuous algorithm) and underspecified algorithm details (step-size rule) reduce clarity and reproducibility.

comments.title: Invalid recursive expression for projected iterates
comments.quote:
By repeatedly applying the same steps, we can reach to the following recursive expression for \( P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i^K) \):

\[
P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i^K) = \left( \prod_{j=0}^{K-1} \frac{(1 - \mu_j^i M c_D)}{\|\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^{j+1}\|_2} \right) P_{S^\perp}(\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^0) - \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \left( \prod_{j=k}^{K-1} \frac{(1 - \mu_j^i M c_D)}{\|\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^{j+1}\|_2} \right) \left( \mu_k^i M P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{e}_{i,k}^\mathbf{O}) + \mu_k^i N P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{e}_{i,k}^\mathbf{X}) \right).
\]
comments.explanation: The derivation leading to (16) assumes that the normalization after each subgradient step can be factored as a product of per-step denominators. However, the unnormalized iterate \(\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^{k+1}\) is a linear combination of the previous iterate and perturbations; its norm is not simply the product of the norms of the intermediate steps. The expression (16) would only hold if the update were purely multiplicative (no additive perturbations), which is not the case here. The subsequent analysis of the discrete case (Theorem 7) depends on this formula, rendering the theoretical results for the finite-sample setting questionable.

comments.title: Theorem 7 condition is incomplete and potentially erroneous
comments.quote:
**Theorem 7** Let \(\mathbf{B}^0 \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times c'}\) with columns randomly sampled from a unit \(\ell_2\) norm spherical distribution where \(c' \ge c\) with \(c\) denoting the true codimension of the inliers subspace \(S\) that satisfies Assumption 1. If

\[
\frac{\sqrt{c'}}{\sqrt{D} - \sqrt{c'-1}} \left( 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{K_*-1} \mu_k ( \eta_{\mathbf{O}} + c_{\mathbf{O},\max} - c_D ) \right) > \sqrt{\frac{c'}{\beta^{K_0+1}}} \left( \max_{i,k} \| \hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^k \|_2 \right) \| \hat{\Delta} \|_2
\]

then \(\operatorname{span}(\hat{\mathbf{B}}^*) \equiv S^\perp\) with probability at least \(1 - \exp\left( -\frac{( \sqrt{D} - \sqrt{c'-1} )^2}{2} \right).\)
comments.explanation: The stated condition has several problems. First, the step-size rule (8) includes a factor \(\mu_0\), but the condition does not reference \(\mu_0\); the sum likely should involve \(\mu_0\) as well. Second, the left-hand side contains \(1 - \sum_{k=0}^{K_*-1} \mu_k (\eta_{\mathbf{O}} + c_{\mathbf{O},\max} - c_D)\); for the inequality to be meaningful, the sum must be less than 1, which imposes constraints not discussed. Third, the right-hand side involves \(\max_{i,k} \|\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^k\|_2\), a random quantity that depends on the entire trajectory, making the condition difficult to verify or interpret. Fourth, the probability bound appears to be a simplistic application of random matrix theory without the usual constants and may not match the dependency on the left-hand side. Moreover, the entire condition is derived from the flawed recursion (16). Consequently, the theorem does not provide a reliable guarantee.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of M and N in continuous algorithm
comments.quote:
Based on (3) and due to Lemma 2, we can now minimize the objective function of the “continuous version” of DPCP by employing a projected subgradient methods (PSGM) that performs the following steps per iteration:

\[
\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^{k+1} = \hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^k - \mu_k^i ( M c_D \hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^k + N c_d P_S(\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^k) ),\quad \mathbf{b}_i^{k+1} = P_{\mathbb{S}^{D-1}}(\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^{k+1}).
\]
comments.explanation: In the continuous setting, the data are described by continuous measures with parameters \(p\) (probability of outlier) and \(1-p\). The finite sample counts \(M\) and \(N\) are not defined. The algorithm should use \(p\) and \(1-p\) (or expected numbers) instead of \(M,N\). This creates confusion about what the 'continuous version' algorithm actually is and whether the subsequent analysis applies to the continuous idealization or only to a specific discrete approximation. The paper later treats the continuous case as having 'benign geometry', but the algorithm statement mixes discrete and continuous notation.

comments.title: Unused variable φ_i in Lemma 2
comments.quote: where \( f_{\mathbf{b}} : \mathbb{S}^{D-1} \to \mathbb{R}, f_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{z}) = |\mathbf{z}^\top \mathbf{b}| \), \(\phi_i\) is the principal angle of \(\mathbf{b}_i\) from the inliers subspace \(S\) and \(p\) is the probability of occurrence of an outlier.
comments.explanation: The variable \(\phi_i\) is introduced in the sentence preceding Lemma 2 but never appears in the equation or elsewhere in the paper. This is a minor oversight that does not affect the technical content but may confuse readers who expect an explicit expression involving \(\phi_i\). The lemma's statement is correct without it, but the redundant definition should be removed.

comments.title: Underspecified step-size rule in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Update the step-size according to a specific rule;
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 instructs to 'Update the step-size according to a specific rule' without specifying which rule or its parameters. While the paper earlier describes a piecewise geometrically diminishing rule (8) with parameters \(\mu_0, \beta, K_0\), the algorithm does not reference this rule, nor does it state how these parameters should be chosen. A reader attempting to reproduce the experiments would need to infer these settings from the text or look up the original DPCP paper. This omission reduces reproducibility.
